QF.id: IV Strategies Using Interest Rate Derivatives_34

question.text: In the context of ETIRD, what does a calendar spread involve?

answer.text: Simultaneous purchase and sale of the same contract type but with different expiry months
Buying a call and selling a put at the same strike and expiry
Taking positions in two different types of bonds
Buying options and selling futures on the same underlying

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A calendar spread (also called time spread) involves taking opposite positions in contracts of the same underlying but with different expiry months - e.g., long June futures and short September futures. **The Trap:** Call-put combination is a synthetic position, not a calendar spread. Different bonds is an inter-commodity spread. Options vs futures is a mixed strategy. **Cross-Exam Context:** Calendar spreads are tested in Series I (currency) and VIII (equity) as well. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Calendar spreads have lower margin requirements than outright positions because the two legs partially offset each other's risk.
